---
author: Tristan Muno
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true
    incremental: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: false
    footer: QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} -- Week 12
    logo: ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
  freeze: true
  cache: true
---

```{r, message = NA}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| eval: true
start_time <- Sys.time()

library(tidyverse) # wrangling and visualization
library(brms) # Bayesian multilevel models via Stan
library(bayesplot) # MCMC diagnostics
library(patchwork) # combine ggplot panels
library(modelsummary) # regression tables
library(ggpubr) # ggpubr theme

col_1 <- "steelblue"
col_2 <- "#E07B39"
col_3 <- "#7B3F9E"
col_4 <- "goldenrod3"
chain_cols <- c(col_1, col_2, col_3, col_4)

# ─── Synthetic cross-national survey ───────────────────────────────────────
# 20 countries, n = 837. Outcome: EU support (0–10). Predictor: income (std).
# Unequal group sizes (15–75) ensure shrinkage is visible for small groups.
# True DGP: α_g ~ N(5, 1.5), β_income = 0.3, σ = 1.0
set.seed(2026)
n_countries <- 20
group_sizes <- c(
  15,
  18,
  20,
  22,
  25,
  28,
  30,
  32,
  35,
  38,
  40,
  45,
  50,
  52,
  55,
  60,
  62,
  65,
  70,
  75
)
n <- sum(group_sizes)
country_id <- rep(seq_len(n_countries), times = group_sizes)
country_lab <- paste0("C", str_pad(seq_len(n_countries), 2, pad = "0"))
alpha_true <- rnorm(n_countries, mean = 5, sd = 1.5)
income <- rnorm(n)
eu_support <- alpha_true[country_id] + 0.3 * income + rnorm(n, 0, 1.0)
eu_support <- pmin(pmax(eu_support, 0), 10)
df <- tibble(eu_support, income, country_id, country = country_lab[country_id])
rm(alpha_true)

# ─── Pre-fit models ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Complete pooling: ignore country structure
fit_pooled <- brm(
  eu_support ~ income,
  data = df,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(5, 2), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)

# Partial pooling: varying intercepts by country
fit_partial <- brm(
  eu_support ~ income + (1 | country),
  data = df,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(5, 2), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma"),
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sd")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)

# No pooling: lm with country dummies (fast, illustrative)
fit_no_pool <- lm(eu_support ~ income + factor(country), data = df)
```

## QMIR 2026 {#sec-title}

::: notes
- Opening.
- Arc: conceptual primer on hierarchical data and partial pooling (~60 min), then brms peek and literature vocabulary connection (~30 min).
- Core message: this is a departure point, not a destination. Not on the exam.
- The shrinkage plot is the click moment of the session — pace toward it.
:::

### Week 12: Multilevel Models & Partial Pooling

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

---

## Week 12 Learning Goals {#sec-goals .smaller}

::: notes
- Read aloud. Three goals map onto the conceptual arc.
- Goal 1: hierarchical data and the independence assumption.
- Goal 2: the three-point spectrum — the core content.
- Goal 3: brms syntax and output reading.
- State the no-exam status explicitly and early.
:::

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**This session is not on the take-home exam.**
It is a primer — your departure point for using multilevel models in future work.
:::

. . .

By the end of today, you should be able to:

1. **Identify hierarchical (nested) data** in IR and EU research and explain why treating grouped observations as independent understates uncertainty
2. **Describe the three-point spectrum** — complete pooling, no pooling, partial pooling — and articulate when each is the right choice
3. **Read the `(1 | group)` formula** in `brms` and interpret the key output quantities ($\hat{\tau}$, $\hat{\sigma}$, group-level intercepts)

---

## What This Session Is (and Is Not) {#sec-framing .smaller}

::: notes
- Say this explicitly and early. Students coming off Week 11 need to know the register has shifted.
- "We are not running the 8-step workflow today. No prior-predictive check, no PPC, no LOO comparison."
- "Think of today as a conceptual map. You are at the edge of what we have covered. The territory ahead is larger."
:::

**This session deliberately breaks the Week 8–11 pattern:**

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.85em"}
| What we do today | What we skip today |
|---|---|
| Hierarchical data intuition | Full 8-step workflow |
| The three-point spectrum | Prior predictive check |
| The shrinkage plot | Posterior predictive check |
| `brms` syntax peek | LOO model comparison |
| FE vocabulary connection | Varying slopes |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.80em"}
Multilevel models are **not on the take-home exam**.
This is your departure point, not your destination — the tools and concepts introduced today are ones you will build on in a follow-up methods seminar, thesis work, or applied research.
:::

# Part 1: Hierarchical Data {#sec-part1}

## Hierarchical Data Is the Rule, Not the Exception {#sec-nested-examples .smaller}

*1.1 What Is Hierarchical Data*

::: notes
- "Before we can talk about models, we need to see the data structure. Almost every dataset you will touch in IR or EU politics has nested structure."
- Go through each row with a concrete example. ESS: 30+ countries, respondents per country are not exchangeable with each other nor with respondents from other countries.
- Emphasize: this is not a niche statistical problem. It is the default shape of comparative politics data.
:::

Almost every dataset in IR and EU politics has individuals nested within groups:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.80em"}
| Structure | Individual unit | Group | Example dataset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cross-national surveys | Respondents | Countries | European Social Survey (ESS) |
| Panel data | Country-years | Countries | V-Dem, World Development Indicators |
| Parliamentary data | Votes / speeches | Parties | ParlGov, Comparative Manifesto Project |
| Conflict data | Dyads | Alliances / regions | UCDP, COW |
| Electoral data | Voters | Districts | CSES |
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**The pattern is always the same**: lower-level units (respondents, country-years, votes) are nested inside higher-level containers (countries, parties, regions).
Observations within the same container tend to be **more similar to each other** than to observations in a different container.
:::

---

## What "Nested" Looks Like {#sec-nesting-diagram .smaller}

::: notes
- Show the figure. "This is our running example for today: 20 synthetic countries, 837 survey respondents. The outcome is EU support on a 0–10 scale."
- "Notice two things. First, the orange diamonds show that countries genuinely differ in their average EU support. Second, the strips have very different widths — some countries have 15 respondents, others 75."
- "These two features — between-group variation and unequal sample sizes — are exactly what make the pooling question interesting."
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-nesting
#| fig-cap: "EU support by country (synthetic cross-national survey, n = 837, 20 countries). Orange diamond = country mean. Dashed line = global mean. Group sizes range from 15 to 75."
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| dpi: 500
df |>
  mutate(country = fct_reorder(country, eu_support, mean)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = country, y = eu_support)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.18, alpha = 0.22, size = 0.8, colour = col_1) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    shape = 18,
    size = 3.2,
    colour = col_2
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = mean(df$eu_support),
    linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "gray50"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7.5)) +
  labs(
    title = "EU Support by Country: Hierarchical Structure",
    subtitle = "Orange diamond = country mean. Countries differ; within-country observations cluster.",
    x = NULL,
    y = "EU Support (0–10)"
  )
```

---

## The Independence Assumption Breaks {#sec-independence-breaks .smaller}

*1.2 Why It Matters*

::: notes
- Callback to Week 8. "We listed four Gauss-Markov assumptions. A4 was independence: the error for observation i tells us nothing about the error for observation j."
- "With nested data, A4 breaks mechanically. Two respondents from Germany share a common country context — their errors are correlated."
- "The consequence: if we ignore this and treat all 837 observations as independent, credible intervals will be too narrow. We understate uncertainty."
- "Intuition: 75 German respondents who share a country context carry less new information than 75 independent draws would. Treating them as independent pretends we have more information than we do."
- No formal derivation. The intuition is enough.
:::

**Week 8 — Gauss-Markov Assumption 4 (Independence):**

$$
\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0 \quad \text{for all } i \neq j
$$ {#eq-a4}

. . .

With nested data, A4 breaks **mechanically**:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- Two ESS respondents from the same country share a common context — their errors are correlated
- 75 same-country respondents carry **less** new information than 75 independent draws
:::

. . .

**If we ignore the nesting:**

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- Credible intervals (and standard errors) are too narrow — we understate uncertainty
- Estimates of cross-level associations (e.g., does country-level income predict EU support?) can be severely biased
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
This is not a Bayesian vs. frequentist problem.
OLS on pooled cross-national data and a Bayesian Gaussian model both face the same issue — the likelihood incorrectly treats within-group observations as independent.
:::

# Part 2: The Three-Point Spectrum {#sec-part2}

## Three Strategies for Grouped Data {#sec-spectrum-overview .smaller}

*2.1 The Three-Point Spectrum*

::: notes
- "When you have grouped data, you have three choices for how to handle the grouping. Think of them as three points on a dial."
- "Each is valid. Each has principled uses. We are not declaring a winner today."
- Introduce the names now; each gets its own slide.
:::

When your data have nested structure, you face a fundamental choice about **how much information to share across groups**:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| Strategy | Information sharing | Group intercepts | Name in applied papers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Complete pooling** | Maximum — share everything | One for all | Pooled OLS / baseline model |
| **No pooling** | Zero — each group independent | Fully separate | Fixed effects (FE) |
| **Partial pooling** | Partial — share, but not completely | Drawn from a common distribution | Random effects / multilevel model |
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**The key question**: how similar are the groups to each other?
Complete pooling assumes identical groups; no pooling assumes fully independent groups; partial pooling lets the data decide.
:::

---

## Strategy 1: Complete Pooling {#sec-complete-pooling .smaller}

::: notes
- "One intercept for all groups. The model does not know that countries exist."
- "Not always wrong. If groups are genuinely exchangeable — e.g., randomised treatment blocks — complete pooling is efficient and appropriate."
- "But if groups differ in their baseline outcomes, complete pooling absorbs all group-level variation into the residual."
:::

**Model:** one intercept $\alpha$ for all groups — the grouping variable is ignored entirely.

$$
y_{ig} \sim \text{Normal}(\alpha + \beta x_{ig},\; \sigma)
$$ {#eq-complete-pool}

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- **Pros**: simple, maximum statistical power, no group-level parameters to estimate
- **Cons**: assumes all group intercepts are identical; if groups differ, estimates are biased and uncertainty is understated
- **When appropriate**: groups are genuinely exchangeable, or group structure is irrelevant to the research question
:::

. . .

In `brms` — the model you already know from Week 8:

```r
brm(eu_support ~ income, data = df, family = gaussian(), ...)
```

---

## Strategy 2: No Pooling {#sec-no-pooling .smaller}

::: notes
- "Separate intercept for each group. Equivalent to adding country dummies to the regression."
- "This is what applied papers call 'country fixed effects'. Each country gets its own constant, estimated from its own data alone."
- "The problem: small groups get wide, unreliable estimates. A country with 15 respondents will have a very uncertain intercept."
- "And there is a subtler problem: the estimates do not talk to each other. Learning that Germany has high EU support tells the model nothing about Poland."
:::

**Model:** separate intercept $\alpha_g$ per group, each estimated independently.

$$
y_{ig} \sim \text{Normal}(\alpha_g + \beta x_{ig},\; \sigma)
$$ {#eq-no-pool}

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- **Pros**: group intercepts absorb any group-level confounders; FE-style identification argument for time-invariant unobservables
- **Cons**: no information sharing — **small groups get wide, unreliable estimates**; cannot include country-level predictors (collinear with group dummies)
- **When appropriate**: FE identification is required (e.g., panel DiD); you genuinely do not want to borrow strength across groups
:::

. . .

Equivalent to adding `factor(country)` as a predictor:

```r
lm(eu_support ~ income + factor(country), data = df)
```

---

## Strategy 3: Partial Pooling {#sec-partial-pooling .smaller}

::: notes
- "The Bayesian-native middle option. Group intercepts are not identical (not complete pooling) and not independent (not no pooling). They are treated as draws from a common distribution whose parameters we also estimate."
- "The key quantity is τ — the SD of the country-intercept distribution. Small τ means countries are similar, estimates shrink toward the global mean. Large τ means countries are very different, estimates stay close to the group-specific data."
- "Crucially, τ is not chosen by the researcher. It is estimated from the data, with its own prior."
:::

**Model:** group intercepts $\alpha_g$ are drawn from a common distribution with estimated parameters.

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{ig} &\sim \text{Normal}(\alpha_g + \beta x_{ig},\; \sigma) \\[4pt]
\alpha_g &\sim \text{Normal}(\mu_\alpha,\; \tau) \\[4pt]
\mu_\alpha,\; \tau &\sim \text{weakly informative priors}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-partial-pool}

. . .

The hyperparameter $\tau$ is the key:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| Value of $\tau$ | Interpretation | Limiting behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau \to 0$ | Groups are essentially identical | Complete pooling |
| $\tau \to \infty$ | Groups are fully independent | No pooling |
| $\tau$ estimated from data | **Partial pooling — data decide** | — |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
$\tau$ is not a tuning parameter — it is a model parameter with its own prior, estimated jointly with all other parameters.
This is the hierarchical prior structure Block II has been building toward: priors can have priors.
:::

---

## The Shrinkage Plot {#sec-shrinkage .smaller}

::: notes
- "This is the click moment. Take a moment to read the three panels."
- "Left: complete pooling — every country gets the same estimate (the global mean). No country-specific information."
- "Middle: no pooling — each country gets its own estimate. Notice the wide intervals for small countries (small points). The estimates are noisy."
- "Right: partial pooling — estimates are pulled toward the global mean, more strongly for small countries. Large countries stay close to their own data; small countries are pulled more."
- "That pulling toward the mean is called shrinkage. It is not a bug — it is what happens when you say 'these countries share a common distribution' and pool information across them."
- "Notice the partial pooling intervals are narrower than no pooling, especially for small groups."
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-shrinkage
#| fig-cap: "The shrinkage plot: three strategies applied to the same data. Point size proportional to country sample size (15–75). Partial pooling pulls small-country estimates toward the global mean (dashed line) more strongly than large-country estimates."
#| fig-height: 5.8
#| dpi: 500
global_mean <- mean(df$eu_support)

no_pool_df <- df |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    est = mean(eu_support),
    se = sd(eu_support) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

partial_df <- ranef(fit_partial)$country[,, "Intercept"] |>
  as_tibble(rownames = "country") |>
  mutate(
    est = Estimate + fixef(fit_partial)["Intercept", "Estimate"],
    lower = Q2.5 + fixef(fit_partial)["Intercept", "Estimate"],
    upper = Q97.5 + fixef(fit_partial)["Intercept", "Estimate"]
  )

country_order <- no_pool_df |> arrange(est) |> pull(country)

shrinkage_df <- bind_rows(
  no_pool_df |>
    transmute(
      country,
      n,
      est = global_mean,
      lower = NA_real_,
      upper = NA_real_,
      model = "Complete Pooling"
    ),
  no_pool_df |>
    transmute(
      country,
      n,
      est,
      lower = est - 1.96 * se,
      upper = est + 1.96 * se,
      model = "No Pooling"
    ),
  partial_df |>
    left_join(no_pool_df |> select(country, n), by = "country") |>
    transmute(country, n, est, lower, upper, model = "Partial Pooling")
) |>
  mutate(
    country = factor(country, levels = country_order),
    model = factor(
      model,
      levels = c("Complete Pooling", "No Pooling", "Partial Pooling")
    )
  )

ggplot(shrinkage_df, aes(x = est, y = country)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = global_mean, linetype = "dashed", colour = "gray55") +
  geom_errorbarh(
    aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper),
    height = 0.3,
    alpha = 0.5,
    colour = "gray40"
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n, colour = model), alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "Complete Pooling" = col_3,
      "No Pooling" = col_2,
      "Partial Pooling" = col_1
    ),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1.2, 4.5), guide = "none") +
  facet_wrap(~model, ncol = 3) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "The Shrinkage Plot: Three Strategies, Same Data",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = global mean. Point size ∝ country n. Small-n countries shrink most.",
    x = "Country-Level EU Support Estimate",
    y = NULL
  )
```

---

## Three Tools, Three Jobs — No Winner {#sec-three-tools .smaller}

::: notes
- "Resist the temptation to say partial pooling is always better. It is not."
- FE line: "If you are doing a DiD design with panel data and need the FE identification argument to absorb time-invariant unobserved confounding, no pooling is the right choice."
- Partial pooling line: "If you want country-specific estimates AND a country-level predictor in the same model, partial pooling is the only option — FE absorbs the country-level predictor into the dummies."
- Complete pooling: "If countries are genuinely exchangeable — e.g., a randomised experiment — complete pooling is efficient and correct."
:::

The three-point spectrum is a **spectrum**, not a ranking:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.80em"}
| Strategy | Use when | Avoid when |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete pooling** | Groups are exchangeable; group-specific estimates not of interest | Group structure is theoretically important |
| **No pooling (FE)** | FE identification needed for unobserved confounders; DiD designs | Groups are small; need to estimate group-level predictors |
| **Partial pooling** | Small or unequal group sizes; group-specific estimates wanted alongside cross-level predictors | FE identification is required for causal inference |
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
What applied papers call **"country fixed effects"** is the no-pooling endpoint of this spectrum.
Partial pooling sits in the middle.
Knowing the spectrum lets you read any paper and identify where its modelling choice sits.
:::

# Part 3: brms Syntax {#sec-part3}

## One Line Changes {#sec-brms-formula .smaller}

*3.1 Fitting a Multilevel Model with brms*

::: notes
- "You already know how to write the first line. The third line is the new one — and it adds exactly one term."
- "(1 | country): the 1 is the random intercept; the | country means 'varying by country'. brms adds the hierarchical prior on α_g and estimates τ automatically."
- Point to the new prior: class = "sd". This is the prior on τ, the SD of the random intercepts.
:::

The only change from Week 8 is `(1 | country)` in the formula:

```r
# Complete pooling — Week 8 style
brm(eu_support ~ income,
    data = df, family = gaussian(), ...)

# No pooling — country dummies
lm(eu_support ~ income + factor(country), data = df)

# Partial pooling — one new term
brm(eu_support ~ income + (1 | country),
    data = df, family = gaussian(), ...)
```

. . .

The `(1 | country)` term tells `brms`:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- Fit a **separate intercept** for each country
- Treat those intercepts as **drawn from a common Normal distribution** $\text{Normal}(\mu_\alpha, \tau)$
- Estimate $\tau$ from the data, with its own prior (`class = "sd"`)
:::

---

## Priors for the Multilevel Model {#sec-priors-mlm .smaller}

::: notes
- "One new prior: class = 'sd' covers τ, the SD of the random intercepts."
- "exponential(1) is a standard weakly informative prior for scale parameters: most mass near zero but allowing larger values. It says 'country intercepts probably do not vary wildly, but I am not ruling it out.'"
- "The fixed-effect priors are unchanged from Week 8."
:::

```{r}
#| label: code-fit-partial
#| eval: false
fit_partial <- brm(
  eu_support ~ income + (1 | country),
  data = df,
  family = gaussian(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(5, 2), class = "Intercept"), # global mean EU support
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"), # income slope
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sigma"), # within-country noise σ
    prior(exponential(1), class = "sd") # between-country SD τ
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| Prior | Parameter | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `normal(5, 2)` on `Intercept` | $\mu_\alpha$ | Global mean EU support, centred on the scale midpoint |
| `normal(0, 1)` on `b` | $\beta$ | Income slope — weakly regularising |
| `exponential(1)` on `sigma` | $\sigma$ | Within-country individual noise |
| `exponential(1)` on `sd` | $\tau$ | Between-country variation in baseline support |
:::

---

## Reading the Output {#sec-brms-output .smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {style="font-size: 0.6em"}
::: notes
- "Two new sections in the output compared to Week 8: Group-Level Effects and Family Specific Parameters."
- "sd(Intercept) = τ̂. If this is large relative to sigma, countries differ substantially in their baseline EU support."
- "The fixed effects (Intercept and income) are the population-level — i.e., average — effects."
- "Individual country intercepts are not shown in the summary. Use ranef() to extract them."
:::
```{r}
#| label: tbl-partial-summary
#| echo: true
summary(fit_partial)
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

**Key quantities to locate:**

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
- **`sd(Intercept)`** = $\hat{\tau}$: estimated SD of country intercepts — how much countries vary in their EU support baseline
- **`sigma`** = $\hat{\sigma}$: within-country residual noise
- **Fixed `income`** = $\hat{\beta}$: population-average income effect, given the data and prior
:::

```{r}
#| echo: true
# Population-level estimates
fixef(fit_partial) |> round(3)
# Variance components: τ and σ
VarCorr(fit_partial)
# Country-specific intercepts
ranef(fit_partial)$country[,, "Intercept"]
```

:::

::::

# Part 4: Connecting to the Literature {#sec-part4}

## "Fixed Effects" in IR/EU Papers {#sec-fe-callout .smaller}

*4.1 Vocabulary Bridge*

::: notes
- "You will encounter this vocabulary constantly. 'We include country fixed effects.' 'We use two-way fixed effects.' 'We cluster standard errors by country.'"
- "Now you have the conceptual map. Country FE = no-pooling endpoint. RE / random intercepts = partial pooling."
- "Both have principled uses. The choice is not about which is more rigorous — it is about what the research question requires."
- "Two-way FE: country FE + year FE simultaneously. This absorbs country-invariant and year-invariant confounders. It is the DiD workhorse in applied IR."
- Important caveat: the "random effects" vocabulary is inconsistent. In some literatures it means partial pooling; in others it means any random component. Check the equation.
:::

You will encounter this vocabulary in every applied IR/EU paper:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| What papers write | What it means | Position on the spectrum |
|---|---|---|
| "Pooled OLS" / baseline model | One intercept for all groups | **Complete pooling** |
| "Country fixed effects" | Separate intercept per country (dummy variables) | **No pooling** |
| "Two-way fixed effects" | Country FE + year FE simultaneously | **No pooling** (two dimensions) |
| "Country random effects" / "random intercepts" | Hierarchical prior on country intercepts | **Partial pooling** |
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
*"What people in the literature call 'fixed effects' is the no-pooling endpoint of this spectrum.
Partial pooling sits in the middle.
Each has principled uses.
We are showing you the spectrum so you can read any paper and know where its choice sits."*
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.76em"}
**The vocabulary is inconsistent across disciplines.**
"Random effects" means partial pooling in econometrics but can mean something different in biostatistics.
When in doubt: find the model equation, not just the label.
:::

# Summary {#sec-summary}

## Learning Goals: Check {#sec-goals-check .smaller}

::: notes
- Read through quickly. Connect each goal back to where it was covered.
- Repeat the no-exam status clearly — some students will have missed the earlier mention.
- Bridge to Week 13 (synthesis week — no new methods content).
:::

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**Reminder: This material is not on the take-home exam.**
Exam scope = Weeks 8–11. Today's session is your departure point for future work.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
By the end of today, you should be able to:

1. ✓ **Identify hierarchical data** in IR/EU research and explain why the independence assumption (Gauss-Markov A4) breaks — and what happens to uncertainty estimates when it is ignored (Part 1)

2. ✓ **Describe the three-point spectrum** — complete pooling, no pooling, partial pooling — and connect the vocabulary to applied papers ("country fixed effects" = no-pooling endpoint; "random intercepts" = partial pooling) (Part 2, Part 4)

3. ✓ **Read the `(1 | country)` formula** in `brms` and interpret `sd(Intercept)` as $\hat{\tau}$, the estimated between-country variation in baseline outcomes (Part 3)
:::

---

## Week 12 Homework: Course Reflection {#sec-homework .smaller}

::: notes
- "This homework is different from every previous week. No Bayesian workflow, no code."
- "I read every response. This is the most useful feedback I can get about the course."
- "30-minute cap — please respect it. I am not asking for a polished essay."
- "No AI. The point is your own thinking, not a generated summary."
- Distribute GitHub Classroom links for Mon and Thu groups separately.
:::

This week's homework is a **course reflection** — not a Bayesian analysis.

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**Format:**

- Passes on **any genuine attempt** — no wrong answers
- **30-minute cap** — please respect it; do not over-engineer
- **No AI use** — your own words only
- Submitted via GitHub Classroom (links posted separately for Mon / Thu)
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
**Prompts (short, open answers — answer as many as you like):**

1. What clicked for you in this course, and when?
2. What did not click — and what would have helped?
3. Which week was hardest, and why?
4. What would you keep, and what would you change about the course design?
5. Are you likely to use any of this beyond the course? In what context?
6. Anything else you want me to know?
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
I read every response. This feedback shapes the next iteration of the course.
:::

---

## References {#sec-references}

::: {style="font-size: 0.80em"}
**Primary reference for partial pooling:**

McElreath, R. (2020). *Statistical Rethinking: A Bayesian Course with Examples in R and Stan* (2nd ed.). CRC Press. **Chapter 13**.

**Secondary reference:**

Gelman, A., & Hill, J. (2006). *Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models*. Cambridge University Press.

**For the formal treatment of PSIS-LOO** (referenced in Part 2 of Week 11):

Vehtari, A., Gelman, A., & Gabry, J. (2017). Practical Bayesian model evaluation using leave-one-out cross-validation and WAIC. *Statistics and Computing*, 27(5), 1413–1432.
:::

```{r}
#| label: session-info
#| eval: true
#| include: false
sessionInfo()
```

```{r}
#| label: exec-time
#| eval: true
#| include: false
Sys.time() - start_time
```
